title: Interpreting Bohm quantum potentials in Computing quantum waves exactly from classical action
abstract: The recent arXiv posting [11], commenting on the paper [7], argues that the proof of Lemma 3.1 in [7] is missing the Bohm quantum potential [1, 2] of the Madelung p.d.e. [9]. This short technical note extends the proof of Lemma 3.1 to introduce a Bohm quantum potential explicitly, and then shows why this term can be assumed to be zero in the wave construction, without loss of generality. The continuity p.d.e. and the Hamilton-Jacobi p.d.e., extended by the Bohm potential, are undisputed. However, the actual action and density solutions depend on their initialization at t = 0. In [7], this initialization is motivated by the Feynman kernel [4], which is fundamentally different from the standard initialization of the Madelung solution [9]. This in turn leads to different action and density solutions, and explains why in one case the Bohm quantum potential disappears and in the other does not. The resulting overall wave, however, is independent of this computational initialization.
summary: This paper provides a mathematical clarification for a method used to compute exact quantum waves from classical action. It proves that while the Bohm quantum potential is present in standard Madelung equations, it vanishes in this specific computational framework without affecting the final quantum wave. This resolves a technical dispute regarding the completeness of the underlying mathematical derivation.
main_problem: The paper addresses a technical critique claiming that a previous proof (Lemma 3.1) omitted the Bohm quantum potential term in the Madelung hydrodynamic equations.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the Bohm quantum potential vanishes in their specific wave construction due to a specific initialization of density and action, and that the resulting overall wave remains independent of this computational initialization.
method: The authors extend the previous proof by explicitly introducing the Bohm potential and using time rescaling to show the potential becomes zero when the Laplace-Beltrami operator is made space-independent.
model_or_system: A mathematical framework for computing exact quantum waves from classical action using the Feynman kernel and Madelung partial differential equations.
key_observables: Action field (phi_j), classical density field (rho_j), and the resulting overall wave (psi).
important_parameters: The ratio of phi/hbar (noted as approximately 5 in a double-slit example); the regime is explicitly noted as being far from the quasi-classical limit.
main_assumptions: The equivalence of operator and product forms of the Schrodinger equation relies on specific action and density initializations; the time transport solution is guaranteed only outside branch points.
paper_structure: The paper begins by addressing a specific technical critique regarding a missing Bohm potential, provides an extended mathematical proof to incorporate the term, explains the mechanism for its vanishing via time rescaling, and concludes by verifying the correctness of the approach using a double-slit example.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
